title: Software & Tools
text:
Facemap
            I developed Facemap for predicting activity of ~50,000 neurons recorded with 2-photon calcium imaging in mice. Facemap predicted activity driven by mouse orofacial movements across different cortical regions. It includes a pose estimation model for tracking distinct keypoints on the mouse face and a neural network model for predicting neural activity using the pose estimates.

            Rastermap
            I also worked on developing GUI for rastermap algorithm. Rastermap is a discovery algorithm for neural data. The algorithm was written by Carsen Stringer and Marius Pachitariu. 

             
        
        GitHub
     
        
        Package
     
        
        Paper
     
        
    
    

    
        
        
            
        
        
        
            Tongue segmentation
            This project focuses on image segmentation of soft-body objects using various convolution neural networks. Specifically, the project uses data from a high-speed imaging camera to perform image segmentation to identify and track a soft-body object, namely the tongue, from static images of mouse faces recorded from a bottom view camera.

title: Research
text:
My research lies at the intersection of systems neuroscience, computation, and behavior. I investigate how unconstrained, spontaneous behaviors shape neural activity in a behaving animal. By developing scalable, open-source computational tools alongside large-scale neural recordings, my work aims to discover behavioral features shaping cortical computation.

Neuroscience
Artificial Intelligence
Deep Learning
Computational Neuroscience
Large-scale data analysis



High-Dimensional Behavior to Neural Activity Prediction
To understand the relationship between spontaneous behavior and neural activity, the field of systems neuroscience required automated pipelines. I developed Facemap, a framework that utilizes deep learning to track, model, and reduce the dimensionality of complex orofacial movements from raw video data.









Facemap framework tracking facial keypoints and neural activity prediction.




Facemap predicts neural activity driven by mouse orofacial movements across cortical regions. It includes pose estimation for tracking facial keypoints and a neural network model that maps behavior to neural responses. The package is implemented in python and utilizes a number of open-source packages, such as pytorch and opencv.




 Nature Neuroscience Paper  Facemap  Documentation  COSYNE Talk









Modeling Behavior via Soft-body Object Recognition
This project focuses on image segmentation of soft-body objects using convolution neural networks. Specifically, the project uses data from a high-speed imaging camera to perform image segmentation to identify and track a soft-body object, i.e. tongue, from static images of mouse faces recorded from a bottom view camera. To perform image segmentation, the project compares different convolution neural networks, including UNet, UNet++, and DeepLabV3, to find a model with the best generalization and test accuracy for the image segmentation task.





Tracking of tongue using segmentation model.









 Project report  Project Code










Research Tools
I employ a variety of methodologies in my research, including:

Data Analysis: Supervised and unsupervised machine learning, deep learning, signal processing techniques
Programming Languages: Python, MATLAB, R, Java
Machine Learning Frameworks: PyTorch, TensorFlow

title: Teaching
text:
I enjoy teaching at the intersection of neuroscience, machine learning, and quantitative methods. My teaching experience includes mentoring students in computational neuroscience as well as undergraduate instruction in chemistry.


Teaching Assistant
Neuromatch NeuroAI Course (2024)

Teaching assistant for an international intensive course on computational neuroscience and artificial intelligence.
Supported a cohort of approximately 15 students through tutorials and discussions.
Assisted students with machine learning concepts and neural data analysis.

title: Atika Syeda
text: I am a PhD student in neuroscience in the joint Johns Hopkins University–HHMI Janelia Research Campus Graduate Program, where I am jointly advised by the Stringer & Pachitariu Lab. My research lies at the intersection of neuroscience and artificial intelligence, with a particular focus on developing machine learning methods to better understand large-scale neural datasets.
